title: The Macro Backdrop: Rates, the Fed, Inflation, Cycle
html:
Every single stock you trade floats on a sea of macro. The same chart that looks like a clean breakout can be quietly fighting a rising tide of interest rates, a hawkish central bank, or a business cycle rolling over. You do not need a PhD in economics to trade, but you do need a working mental model of the four big forces: interest rates, Fed policy, inflation, and the business cycle. This lesson builds that model and shows how it feeds directly into valuations.

Why interest rates set the price of everything

A stock is a claim on future cash flows. To value those cash flows today, you discount them back at some rate. The discount rate rises and falls with prevailing interest rates, anchored by the yield on government bonds (in the US, the 10-year Treasury). When the discount rate goes up, the present value of distant cash flows goes down — and the further out the cash flow, the more it shrinks.

Definition.
Present value of a future cash flow: PV = CF ÷ (1 + r)t, where r is the discount rate and t is years out. A high-growth company earns most of its profits far in the future (large t), so it is extremely sensitive to changes in r.

This is why a small move in rates can crush long-duration assets like unprofitable tech, while barely scratching a utility that pays a steady dividend today. Growth stocks are long-duration; value stocks and cash-rich businesses are short-duration.

Worked example: discount rate compresses a growth multiple
NOVA earns $10/yr ten years out. Value just that cash flow. At r = 2%: PV = 10 ÷ (1.02)10 = $8.20 At r = 5%: PV = 10 ÷ (1.05)10 = $6.14 The same $10 lost 25% of its present value from a 3-point rate rise. A cash flow due NEXT year barely moves: 10/1.02 = 9.80 vs 10/1.05 = 9.52, only -2.9%.

The Fed: the hand on the rate dial

In the US, the Federal Reserve sets the federal funds rate — the overnight rate banks charge each other. Everything else (mortgages, corporate borrowing, the discount rate in your DCF) keys off that anchor. The Fed has a dual mandate: stable prices (low inflation) and maximum employment. When inflation runs hot, the Fed hikes rates to cool demand. When the economy weakens and unemployment rises, it cuts to stimulate.

A hiking cycle is the classic headwind for stocks, especially expensive ones. The 2022 episode is the textbook case: the Fed took the funds rate from near zero to over 5% in about 18 months. Long-duration growth multiples compressed hard while the underlying businesses were still growing — the price fell because the discount rate rose.

Insight.
Watch the second derivative. Markets price the expected path of rates, not today's level. A hike that was fully expected can rally stocks if the Fed signals it is the last one. "Don't fight the Fed" really means: don't fight the direction of the policy path.

Inflation: the fuel for the dial

Inflation is the rate at which prices rise, tracked by gauges like CPI and the Fed's preferred PCE. Moderate inflation is healthy. Runaway inflation forces the Fed to hike, which lifts discount rates and hurts valuations. Deflation (falling prices) is worse in a different way — it signals collapsing demand and crushes earnings. Traders watch the monthly CPI print like a sporting event because it shapes the rate path.

The business cycle: the tide underneath

Economies breathe in a cycle of four phases. Different assets and sectors lead in each.

PhaseWhat's happeningRates / FedTends to lead
Early recoveryEconomy bottoms, growth resumesLow, cuttingCyclicals, financials, small caps
Mid expansionStrong growth, rising earningsNeutral, starting to hikeTech, industrials
Late cycleOverheating, inflation risingHiking, restrictiveEnergy, materials
RecessionContraction, layoffsHigh then cuttingDefensives, staples, utilities, bonds

The chart below is a stylized index moving through these phases. Notice how the character of price changes — the early-recovery launch off the lows, the smooth mid-cycle trend, the choppy late-cycle top, and the rollover.

Early in the move price launches off a base with the 50-week MA crossing back above the 200 — a golden cross that often coincides with early recovery. Mid-cycle the trend is orderly and pullbacks are shallow. Late cycle the advance gets choppy and narrow, and the eventual rollover drags the 50 back below the 200.

Pitfall.
The cycle is not a clock. Phases stretch and compress, and the market front-runs the economy by 6-9 months. Don't sell every stock because a recession headline appeared — stocks often bottom while the news is still terrible.
▸Try It

Map today's macro backdrop to a cycle phase.

  1. Find the current fed funds rate and the last three CPI prints. Are rates rising, flat, or falling?
  2. Is the Fed signaling more hikes, a pause, or cuts? Note the expected path, not just the level.
  3. Using the cycle table, decide which phase best fits and which sectors it favors.
  4. Pick one growth stock and one cash-rich value stock. Predict which is more exposed if rates rise another point, and explain why using the duration idea.

title: Sector Rotation and Relative Strength
html:
The market is not one thing. Under the surface of the S&P 500 sit eleven sectors, and money is constantly migrating between them as the cycle turns and expectations shift. That migration is sector rotation, and the tool that makes it visible is relative strength — comparing one thing's performance against another rather than looking at price in isolation. Master this and you stop asking "is this stock going up?" and start asking the better question: "is it going up faster than the alternatives?"

The eleven sectors and their personalities

The standard GICS sectors split into broad temperaments. Cyclicals — consumer discretionary, financials, industrials, materials, technology — boom when the economy is strong and growth is being priced in. Defensives — consumer staples, utilities, healthcare — hold up when the economy weakens because people keep buying toothpaste, electricity, and medicine no matter what. Energy and real estate march to their own commodity- and rate-driven drums.

TypeSectors (ETF)Shines when
CyclicalDiscretionary (XLY), Financials (XLF), Industrials (XLI), Tech (XLK)Growth accelerating, rates manageable
DefensiveStaples (XLP), Utilities (XLU), Healthcare (XLV)Growth slowing, fear rising
Inflation-sensitiveEnergy (XLE), Materials (XLB)Late cycle, rising commodity prices

Reading relative strength with a ratio chart

A relative-strength (RS) ratio is one price divided by another. If you plot XLY (discretionary) ÷ XLP (staples), a rising line means risk-on cyclicals are beating defensives — the market wants offense. A falling line means defense is winning — caution is creeping in. The ratio can trend, break out, and form patterns just like a price chart, but it carries cleaner information about where money is going.

Definition.
Relative strength ratio = PriceA ÷ PriceB. Trend up → A is outperforming B. The ABSOLUTE level is meaningless; only the DIRECTION and the trendline matter.

The chart below is the discretionary-over-staples ratio coming out of a bottom — the classic signature of early recovery as money rotates from defensives back into cyclicals.

Notice this is a ratio, not a price. The breakout above the prior shelf says discretionary stocks are now gaining ground on staples day after day. Even if the broad index were flat, this rotation tells you the internal leadership has flipped to offense — a tailwind for cyclical names and a reason to be skeptical of being overweight utilities here.

The rotation map across the cycle

Sam Stovall's classic sector-rotation model layers the sectors onto the business cycle. Early in a recovery, leadership runs through interest-rate-sensitive cyclicals (financials, discretionary, then tech and industrials). As the expansion matures and inflation rises, leadership rotates to materials and energy. As the economy tops and rolls into recession, money flees to staples, utilities, and healthcare — and into bonds.

Reading two ratios together
Risk appetite: XLY ÷ XLP rising → offense (cyclicals leading) Risk appetite: XLY ÷ XLP falling → defense (staples leading) Cycle stage: XLE ÷ SPY rising → late-cycle inflation theme Stress check: XLU ÷ SPY rising → flight to safety / risk-off Confirm with the absolute trend before acting — a ratio can rise simply because B is falling faster.

From sectors to single stocks

RS is fractal. Just as you rank sectors against the index, you rank stocks against their sector. The cleanest setups stack the strength: a strong stock, in a strong sector, in a strong market. If ACME is making new RS highs versus XLK and XLK is making new RS highs versus SPY, you have a name being pulled higher by two tailwinds at once. The weakest setups invert this — a stock you like that is quietly underperforming its own sector is fighting an undertow.

Insight.
Relative strength leads price. A stock that refuses to fall during a market pullback — holding flat while the index drops — is building relative strength that often translates into outsized gains when the index turns back up. Watch what won't go down.
Pitfall.
A rising ratio is not automatically bullish for A. If both fall but B falls faster, the ratio rises while you lose money owning A. Always check the absolute chart alongside the ratio.
▸Try It

Build a simple rotation dashboard.

  1. Pull charts for XLY, XLP, XLE, XLU and the index (SPY).
  2. Create the ratio XLY:XLP. Is it rising or falling over the last three months? What does that say about risk appetite?
  3. Create XLE:SPY and XLU:SPY. Do they suggest late-cycle or risk-off conditions?
  4. Pick the sector that looks strongest, find a stock inside it, and check whether that stock is also outperforming its sector ETF. Note whether the strength stacks or conflicts.

title: Market Breadth and Sentiment
html:
The index level tells you what the average stock did, but averages lie. A market can grind to new highs while the majority of its members are quietly falling apart, propped up by a handful of giants. Breadth measures how many stocks are actually participating, and sentiment measures how greedy or fearful the crowd is. Together they let you see whether a move is broad and healthy or narrow and fragile — and whether the crowd is leaning so hard one way that the trade is crowded.

Breadth: who is actually participating

The cleanest breadth tool is the advance-decline (A/D) line: a running cumulative total of (advancing stocks minus declining stocks) each day. When the A/D line makes new highs alongside the index, the rally is broad and trustworthy. When the index makes a new high but the A/D line does not — a breadth divergence — fewer and fewer names are doing the lifting. That is a warning, not a sell signal, but historically the nastiest tops show this fingerprint.

Definition.
Cumulative A/D line: today's value = yesterday's value + (advancers − decliners). Other breadth gauges: % of stocks above their 200-day MA, and new highs minus new lows. All answer one question: how many soldiers are marching, not just the generals?

The chart below shows the danger case described in the lesson skill: price pushing to a new high while breadth and volatility quietly disagree.

Read the panes together. Up top, price prints a marginally higher high. Below, the oscillator rolls over to a lower high — the momentum and participation behind the move are draining even as the headline number ticks up. Treat this as a fragile market: tighten stops, reduce size, and demand more from new long setups. Divergences can persist for weeks, so this is a posture change, not an instant short.

Sentiment: the VIX and the put/call ratio

The VIX is the market's expected 30-day volatility implied by S&P 500 options — the "fear gauge." A low VIX (say, low teens) signals complacency; a spiking VIX signals panic. Crucially, the VIX tends to move inverse to stocks. A rally accompanied by a rising VIX is internally contradictory and often unstable — options traders are paying up for protection even as the index climbs.

The put/call ratio compares put volume to call volume. High readings mean traders are buying lots of downside protection (fear); low readings mean everyone is buying calls (greed). Both VIX and put/call are best read as contrarian extremes: peak fear often marks bottoms, and peak greed often marks tops.

Sentiment cheat sheet
VIX low (<15): complacency — protection is cheap, crowd relaxed VIX spiking (>30): fear / capitulation — often near short-term bottoms Put/Call high (>1.1): excess fear — contrarian bullish tilt Put/Call low (<0.7): excess greed — contrarian bearish tilt Rule of thumb: be greedy when others are fearful, fearful when others are greedy — but only at EXTREMES.

The fragile-rally pattern: putting it together

The textbook warning configuration combines all three signals: the index rallies, the A/D line falls (narrow participation), and the VIX rises instead of falling (rising hedging demand). Each alone is noise; together they say the move is being driven by a few mega-caps while the broad tape and the options market quietly brace for trouble.

Insight.
Breadth thrusts work the other way too. When a market launches off a bottom and 90% of stocks advance for several days, that overwhelming participation (a "breadth thrust") is one of the most reliable bullish signals there is. Healthy beginnings are broad; dangerous endings are narrow.
Pitfall.
Sentiment extremes can get more extreme. A high VIX can spike higher; a low put/call can stay low for months in a strong bull. Use these as a thumb on the scale and a reason to manage risk, never as a standalone timing trigger.
▸Try It

Audit the current market's internal health.

  1. Pull the index, its cumulative A/D line, and the VIX on the same dates.
  2. At the last index high, did the A/D line confirm with its own high, or diverge?
  3. Was the VIX falling (healthy) or rising (fragile) into that high?
  4. Check the put/call ratio. Is the crowd fearful or greedy right now?
  5. Write a one-sentence verdict: is the current advance broad and healthy, or narrow and fragile?

title: A Primer on ETFs and Indices
html:
Most traders interact with the market through indices and ETFs before they ever buy a single stock. An index is a recipe — a rules-based measurement of a basket. An ETF (exchange-traded fund) is a tradable product that holds that basket and trades on an exchange like a stock. Understanding how they are built and weighted explains why "the market" can rise while most stocks fall, and why two funds tracking the same theme can perform very differently.

What an index actually is

An index is just a formula applied to a list of constituents. The S&P 500 is the 500-ish largest US companies, weighted by market value. The Nasdaq-100 is the largest non-financial names on the Nasdaq. The Dow is an oddball — 30 stocks weighted by price, a quirk from 1896. An index itself is not investable; you cannot buy "the S&P 500." You buy a fund that tracks it.

Weighting: the most important and least understood detail

How constituents are weighted decides everything about behavior.

SchemeHow weight is setConsequence
Cap-weightedBy market value (price × shares)Mega-caps dominate; a few names drive returns
Equal-weightedEvery stock gets the same %Small and mid names matter equally; needs rebalancing
Price-weightedBy share price (the Dow)A high-priced stock outweighs a bigger company

The dominant scheme is cap weighting, and it has a profound effect: in a cap-weighted S&P 500 fund, the largest handful of mega-caps can account for a third or more of the entire fund. When those few giants rip, the index rips — even if the other ~490 stocks are flat or down.

Worked example: a few mega-caps dominate the move
Toy 5-stock cap-weighted index. Weight = each company's value ÷ total. MEGA $2,000B weight = 2000/3000 = 66.7% BIG $ 500B weight = 500/3000 = 16.7% MID $ 300B weight = 300/3000 = 10.0% SMALL $ 150B weight = 150/3000 = 5.0% TINY $ 50B weight = 50/3000 = 1.7% If MEGA rises +10% and ALL four others fall -5%: index = (.667)(+10) + (.333)(-5) = +6.67 − 1.67 = +5.0% The index rose 5% while 4 of 5 stocks FELL.

This is exactly the "narrow market" dynamic from the breadth lesson. The fix when you want to see the average stock is an equal-weight ETF (like RSP for the S&P 500): comparing the cap-weighted index to its equal-weight version is itself a breadth gauge. When cap-weight runs far ahead of equal-weight, leadership is dangerously concentrated.

How an ETF works mechanically

An ETF is a wrapper. The fund holds the actual basket of stocks. Large institutions called authorized participants can create or redeem big blocks of ETF shares by swapping them for the underlying basket. This creation/redemption mechanism is the magic that keeps the ETF's market price glued to its net asset value (NAV) — if the ETF trades above NAV, APs arbitrage the gap away. That is why a well-run ETF tracks its index closely and trades all day at a fair price, unlike a mutual fund that prices once at the close.

Definition.
NAV = (total value of holdings − liabilities) ÷ shares outstanding. Arbitrage by authorized participants keeps ETF market price ≈ NAV. The small gap between them is the premium/discount.

Flavors and the fine print

ETFs come in many types: broad index, sector (the XL- family), thematic, bond, commodity, and leveraged/inverse. The leveraged ones (2x, 3x, inverse) reset daily and suffer from volatility decay — over time their compounding diverges badly from the multiple of the index's longer-run return. They are short-term trading tools, not buy-and-hold vehicles.

This is what a healthy index ETF looks like: a persistent uptrend with price holding above a rising 50-day, itself above a rising 200-day. But remember the lesson above — this smooth advance could be driven by a few mega-caps. Always check the equal-weight cousin to know whether the whole army is marching or just the generals.

Pitfall.
Two ETFs with similar names can hold very different baskets and charge very different fees. Always check the top-10 holdings, the weighting scheme, and the expense ratio before assuming a fund does what its ticker implies.
Insight.
The cap-weight vs equal-weight spread is a free breadth indicator. When the equal-weight ETF starts outperforming the cap-weight version, leadership is broadening — often a healthy sign after a narrow, top-heavy rally.
▸Try It

Dissect an index fund.

  1. Look up the top 10 holdings of a major cap-weighted S&P 500 ETF and add their weights. What fraction of the fund is just those 10?
  2. Pull the cap-weighted ETF and its equal-weight cousin (e.g. SPY vs RSP) over the past year. Which outperformed, and what does that say about breadth?
  3. Find a 3x leveraged ETF and compare its 1-year return to 3× the index's 1-year return. Explain the gap using volatility decay.

title: A Primer on Futures
html:
Futures are the plumbing of the financial world. They are standardized contracts to buy or sell something at a set price on a future date, traded on exchanges with deep liquidity and built-in leverage. Index futures (like the E-mini S&P 500) trade nearly 24 hours and are where the overnight 'gap' is decided before the cash market even opens. You do not need to trade them to benefit from understanding them — but their mechanics of leverage, margin, and roll are essential macro literacy.

What a futures contract is

A futures contract obligates the buyer (long) to take delivery and the seller (short) to deliver a specified quantity of an asset at a fixed price on a set expiration. In practice almost all financial futures are cash-settled or closed before delivery — nobody actually wants 1,000 barrels of oil. The contract is standardized by the exchange: the underlying, the size, the tick, and the expiry are all fixed, so the only thing traders negotiate is price.

Definition.
Notional value = contract multiplier × index price. For the E-mini S&P 500, the multiplier is $50/point. At an index of 5,000, one contract controls 50 × 5,000 = $250,000 of exposure — controlled with a margin deposit far smaller than that.

Leverage and margin: the core mechanic

You do not pay the full notional. You post initial margin — a good-faith deposit, often just a few percent of notional. This is the source of futures' enormous leverage. Gains and losses are settled daily through mark-to-market: every evening your account is credited or debited for the day's move. If your balance falls below the maintenance margin, you get a margin call and must add funds or be liquidated.

Worked example: E-mini S&P leverage and P&L
One E-mini S&P contract, $50 per index point. Index level: 5,000 points Notional controlled: 50 × 5,000 = $250,000 Initial margin (~5%): ≈ $13,000 Index rises 20 points to 5,020: P&L: 20 pts × $50 = +$1,000 Return on margin: 1,000 / 13,000 = +7.7% from a +0.4% index move The SAME move down: P&L: −20 pts × $50 = −$1,000 → −7.7% on margin Leverage cuts both ways — a 0.4% index move is a 7.7% swing on capital.

That table is the whole lesson in risk: a tiny index move becomes a large move on your posted capital. This is why position sizing in futures is measured in ticks and dollars at risk, never in vague gut feel.

The roll, and the curve: contango vs backwardation

Futures expire. To hold a position past expiry you roll — close the expiring contract and open the next month. The relationship between contract months forms the futures curve. When further-out contracts are more expensive than near ones, the market is in contango. When they are cheaper, it is in backwardation.

Curve shapeFar month vs nearRoll cost for a longTypical cause
ContangoFar is higherNegative (roll yield bleeds)Storage/carry costs, calm supply
BackwardationFar is lowerPositive (roll yield helps)Tight supply, scarcity premium
Pitfall.
In persistent contango, a long that keeps rolling sells the cheap expiring contract and buys the pricier next month each cycle — a slow bleed. This is exactly why commodity ETFs that hold futures (oil, VIX) can decay relentlessly even when spot prices are flat. Roll yield is invisible on the price chart but very real in your account.

Why traders watch index futures even if they never trade them

Because they trade overnight, S&P futures price in news while the cash market is closed. The pre-market move you see in stock index quotes is really the futures move. Watching the futures gap and any breakout levels gives a read on the day's likely open. The chart below shows an overnight index-futures gap up and follow-through — the kind of move that sets the tone before the bell.

Read it as a sentiment tell: the gap reflects news absorbed while stocks were closed, and whether price holds above the gap or fills it back in often frames the entire session's bias. A gap that holds and extends signals real demand; a gap that immediately fills warns the move lacked conviction.

Insight.
Futures are pure expression of macro views: rates (Treasury futures), inflation (commodities), risk appetite (equity index). Even as a stock trader, glancing at the bond and equity futures curve each morning tells you the macro weather before you place a single equity trade.
▸Try It

Practice futures math and curve reading.

  1. Take the current S&P index level. Compute the E-mini notional ($50 × level) and estimate margin at ~5%.
  2. Suppose the index moves 30 points your way. Compute the dollar P&L and the return on that margin. Repeat for a 30-point move against you.
  3. Look up the front two months of a commodity future (e.g. oil). Is the curve in contango or backwardation? State whether a long roll helps or hurts.
  4. Before the next session, note where S&P futures are trading pre-market versus the prior close, and predict whether the open gaps up or down.

title: A Primer on Forex
html:
The foreign exchange market is the largest, most liquid market on earth — trillions of dollars a day, trading 24 hours across global sessions. Even if you never trade currencies, FX is the connective tissue of macro: a strong dollar reshapes commodity prices, corporate earnings, and capital flows worldwide. This lesson teaches you to read a currency pair, count pips, and understand the single biggest driver of FX: interest-rate differentials.

Reading a currency pair

Currencies always trade in pairs because a currency only has value relative to another. A quote like EUR/USD = 1.0900 means one euro (the base currency, listed first) buys 1.0900 US dollars (the quote currency, listed second). If the pair rises, the base is strengthening against the quote. If it falls, the base is weakening. So EUR/USD going up means the euro is getting stronger / the dollar weaker.

Definition.
In BASE/QUOTE, the number is how many units of QUOTE buy one unit of BASE. Pair up → base strengthens. Pair down → base weakens (quote strengthens).

Pips: the unit of movement

A pip ('percentage in point') is the standard smallest increment for most pairs — the fourth decimal place, or 0.0001. Pairs involving the yen are quoted to two decimals, so a JPY pip is 0.01. Pips let traders speak about moves precisely and size positions consistently.

Worked example: counting pips and P&L on EUR/USD
Move: 1.0850 → 1.0900 Pip count: (1.0900 − 1.0850) / 0.0001 = 50 pips Position: 1 standard lot = 100,000 units of base. Pip value (USD quote): 100,000 × 0.0001 = $10 per pip P&L on the move (long EUR): 50 × $10 = +$500 A mini lot (10,000) = $1/pip → +$50; a micro lot (1,000) = $0.10/pip → +$5.

So the example move from the lesson skill — EUR/USD 1.0850 to 1.0900 — is exactly a 50-pip rise: the euro strengthened, the dollar weakened, and a long-euro standard lot made $500. That tidy arithmetic is why FX traders think in pips and lots rather than percentages.

What actually drives currencies: rate differentials

The dominant long-run driver of major FX pairs is the interest-rate differential between the two countries. Capital flows toward higher yields. If the Fed is hiking while the European Central Bank holds steady, dollar-denominated assets pay more, capital flows into dollars, and EUR/USD falls (dollar strengthens). When the ECB starts catching up — narrowing the gap — flows reverse and EUR/USD rises. This is the FX echo of the rates lesson that opened this part.

Rate-differential driver of EUR/USD
Fed hikes, ECB holds: US yield > EU yield → capital → USD → EUR/USD falls ECB catches up: gap narrows → flows reverse → EUR/USD rises The 50-pip move: 1.0850 → 1.0900 on a surprise dovish Fed / hawkish ECB shift Other drivers stacked on top: growth, inflation, trade balances, risk sentiment (safe-haven flows to USD/JPY/CHF).

Layered on rates are growth differentials, inflation, trade balances, and risk sentiment. In risk-off panics, capital rushes to safe havens — the US dollar, Japanese yen, and Swiss franc — regardless of yield. So FX is simultaneously a rate-differential machine and a fear gauge.

This downtrend is the rate-differential story drawn as a chart: as US yields pull ahead of European yields, capital flows into dollars and the pair grinds lower beneath falling moving averages. A trader reading FX this way watches central-bank meetings and yield spreads the way an equity trader watches earnings — they are the catalysts that bend the trend.

Insight.
The dollar is the world's gravity. A strong dollar (rising DXY index) tightens global financial conditions, pressures commodities priced in dollars, and squeezes emerging markets that borrowed in dollars. Even a pure stock trader should glance at the dollar trend — it quietly tilts the whole field.
Pitfall.
FX leverage is extreme (often 30:1 or more), and the market runs 24 hours, so positions can move violently while you sleep on a central-bank headline. The same rate-differential logic that creates the trend can reverse it in seconds on a surprise.
▸Try It

Read a pair and tie it to rates.

  1. Quote EUR/USD right now. Which is base, which is quote, and which currency is stronger than last month?
  2. Pick any 40-pip move and compute the P&L on a standard, mini, and micro lot.
  3. Compare the current US and Eurozone policy rates. Which side has the higher yield, and does the recent EUR/USD direction agree with the rate-differential logic?
  4. Pull the dollar index (DXY). Is the dollar strong or weak, and name one commodity or sector that direction would pressure.

title: How Macro and Other Markets Feed Back to Stocks
html:
You have now toured the macro backdrop, sector rotation, breadth and sentiment, ETFs, futures, and forex. This final lesson closes the loop: it shows how all of that feeds back down into a decision about a single stock. Great traders work top-down — market, then sector, then stock — so the macro tailwind or headwind is built into the thesis from the start, not discovered after the trade goes wrong.

The top-down funnel

Think of every single-stock decision as passing through four filters. A high-quality setup aligns all four; the more that conflict, the more the trade is swimming upstream.

  1. Macro — rate path, Fed stance, cycle phase. Tailwind or headwind for risk assets?
  2. Market — is the index trending up with healthy breadth and a calm VIX?
  3. Sector — is the stock's sector outperforming on relative strength?
  4. Stock — does the individual chart and fundamental story hold up?
Definition.
Top-down analysis: filter from the largest force (macro) to the smallest (the single stock), keeping only setups where the layers reinforce rather than fight each other. Alignment stacks the odds; conflict subtracts edge.

A worked thesis: the exporter under a strong dollar

Take the scenario from this lesson's skill. You like ACME, a multinational exporter that earns most of its revenue overseas. The bottom-up story is clean: good products, rising sales, a tidy chart. Then the macro shifts — the Fed signals higher-for-longer, the 10-year yield rises, and the dollar strengthens. Each macro thread, traced down, reshapes the ACME thesis.

Tracing macro into the single-stock thesis
Rising 10-yr yield: discount rate up → compresses ACME's growth multiple Strong dollar: overseas revenue worth fewer USD → FX translation headwind Strong dollar: ACME's goods pricier abroad → competitiveness hit Sector check: is its sector still outperforming SPY? → confirm RS Market check: breadth + VIX healthy? → confirm regime Net: a clean bottom-up chart now carries two fresh macro headwinds. Smaller size, tighter stop, or wait.

Nothing about ACME's products changed. But the price of its future earnings (via the discount rate) and the dollar value of its foreign revenue both deteriorated because of forces two and three filters up. A trader who only looked at the stock chart would miss why the breakout keeps failing. A top-down trader saw the headwind coming.

The chart shows the tension: the bottom-up setup wants to break out above resistance, but the macro ceiling — rising yields compressing the multiple, a strong dollar eroding revenue — keeps capping it. When the stock-level signal and the macro-level signal disagree, the larger force usually wins over time. That is the whole reason to work top-down.

The intermarket web

The markets you studied in this part are wired together. Rising yields lift the dollar and pressure long-duration growth stocks and gold. A strong dollar pressures commodities and emerging markets. A spiking VIX coincides with bonds and the yen catching safe-haven bids. None of these moves in isolation — and reading the web gives you context no single stock chart can.

If this rises…Usual pressure on…Usual tailwind for…
10-year yieldGrowth stocks, gold, utilitiesBanks, the dollar
US dollar (DXY)Commodities, exporters, EMImporters, dollar-cost buyers
VIXCyclicals, high-beta namesDefensives, bonds, yen
OilAirlines, consumersEnergy sector
Insight.
You do not need to forecast macro — you need to be positioned consistently with it. Knowing that rising yields hurt your growth name lets you size smaller, hedge, or simply wait for the macro to turn, rather than being blindsided.
Pitfall.
Don't let macro become an excuse to ignore your stops. The macro view sets posture and size; the chart still triggers entries and exits. A trader who marries a macro narrative and overrides every price signal will hold losers far too long. Use macro as a tilt, the chart as the trigger.
▸Try It

Run one stock through the full top-down funnel.

  1. Pick a stock you are watching. Note whether most of its revenue is domestic or international.
  2. Macro: what is the rate path and cycle phase? Tailwind or headwind for this kind of stock?
  3. Market and sector: is the index trending with healthy breadth, and is the stock's sector outperforming on relative strength?
  4. Intermarket: check the 10-year yield and the dollar. Do they help or hurt this specific name's thesis?
  5. Write a verdict: do all four filters align, or is the stock fighting an upstream force? Decide whether to size up, size down, or wait.
